query_text: patents about event participants getting personalized information during a live event
evaluation_goal: Do the top patents look on-topic? Does optimized mode produce a more useful explanation than baseline?
baseline_answer:
I’m checking recent and older patent records on live-event attendee personalization, not just generic recommendation systems. I’ll pull the closest patent families, then narrow to the ones that actually involve participant-specific information delivered during the event.

I’ve got a few candidates, but some are noise: generic event platforms and pre-event recommendation patents are not really your target. I’m tightening on patents that combine attendee profiles, live delivery, and context-specific information at the venue or during a meeting.

Thought for 28s
Closest hits I found:

WO2020033354A3 / related US family — “Methods and systems for personalizing visitor experience at a venue.”
This is one of the stronger matches. It is not framed as a generic event app; it explicitly captures visitor data such as location and demographics from mobile devices, cameras, sensors, and beacons, feeds that into a recommendation engine, and uses the output to provide personalized recommendations, multimedia content, and digital guides during the venue experience. That is very close to “participants getting personalized information during a live event,” especially for museums, zoos, and similar physical venues.

US9659102B1 — “Event application.”
Also a pretty relevant match. It covers an event-specific app that delivers event information to a user device, pulls information from multiple sources via APIs, and filters displayed information using a user profile. It is weaker than the venue-personalization family above because it reads more like an event-information delivery platform than a deep attendee-context engine, but it does hit multi-source event info plus user-profile filtering during the event window.

US20070058041A1 / US8051452B2 family — “System and methods for enhancing the experience of spectators attending a live sporting event, with contextual information distribution capability.”
This is older, but still highly on-point for live events. It expressly delivers venue or live-event contextual information to spectators’ handheld devices at the venue. More importantly, parts of the family describe profile-based content management, where generic content is filtered into spectator-specific content based on age, preferences, or how much contextual information the spectator wants. That is a real live-event personalization angle, not just a broadcast feed.

US20250037527A1 — “Mobile badge for access authorization at multi-session events.”
Relevant if your angle includes access control or role-based information access. It builds and updates a user profile from ID, location, and time data, then uses that profile plus contextual information to determine access to event sessions via a mobile badge or dynamic electronic ticket. Strong on participant profile + dynamic authorization, but weaker on delivering rich personalized informational content.

US20250292778A1 — “Near real-time in-meeting content item suggestions.”
This is more meeting-centric than physical live-event-centric, but conceptually very close if your invention involves responding to live attendee utterances with relevant personalized content. It detects attendee utterances in real time, uses contextual information and attendee/user profiles, and ranks content items for presentation during the meeting. Good prior art if your claims extend to conferences, live sessions, or interactive meetings.

My blunt ranking for your exact phrasing, “event participants getting personalized information during a live event”:

Best overall conceptual match: WO2020033354A3 venue-personalization family.

Best older live-event/spectator match: US20070058041A1 / US8051452B2 family.

Best app-based multi-source event-info match: US9659102B1.

Best if access rights matter: US20250037527A1.

Best if the “event” is really a meeting/session with live conversational prompts: US20250292778A1.

What looks less useful:

Generic social-event recommendation systems are mostly pre-event recommendation noise.

Broad event-management platforms are usually too high level unless your claims are also broad and operational.

If you’re trying to assess novelty for a draft claim set, the real pressure points are probably:

attendee/participant profile generation

multiple source aggregation

live or in-event delivery

contextual request handling

access control / authorization over what a participant can receive

If you want, I can do the next useful step: narrow these to the ones closest to an invention that combines participant profiles + live contextual information requests + access-controlled delivery.

query_text: patents about participant profiles and contextual information at physical gatherings
evaluation_goal: Do the top patents stay anchored on physical-gathering profile generation and contextual information retrieval?
baseline_answer:
I’m narrowing this to physical gatherings, so I’m filtering out generic online meeting patents and broad event-software fluff. The target is profile-driven information delivery tied to people at an in-person venue.

I’ve got the strongest venue-side matches. One weak spot is that a lot of patents talk about “visitors” or “spectators” rather than “participants,” but functionally that is the same lane if your claims are about people physically present and receiving profile-driven contextual info.

Thought for 22s
Best matches for participant profiles + contextual information at physical gatherings:

1. WO2020033354A3 / related US family — “Methods and systems for personalizing visitor experience at a venue.”
This is the strongest fit. It is squarely about a physical venue and explicitly says personalized visitor information, including location and demographic information, can be captured from mobile devices, cameras, sensors, and wireless beacons, then fed into a recommendation engine. The same family includes continuations on personalized multimedia, supplemental media, ticketing, venue management, and location-sequence recommendations, which makes it broad prior art around profile-driven in-venue personalization.

2. US20180352373A1 — “Personalized itinerary generation and mapping system.”
Also strong. It generates a personalized itinerary and map for a user within a venue, with recommendations based on both the user’s profile information and point-of-interest information, plus the user’s current location relative to the venue. That is very close if your concept involves contextual info tied to where the participant is during a physical gathering.

3. US7212983B2 — “Method and apparatus for providing visitors with a personalized itinerary and managed access to attractions.”
Older, but relevant. It covers creating a personalized itinerary for visitors to a facility such as a zoo, theme park, historic area, or shopping district, and also includes managed access to attractions. It is more itinerary/access oriented than live contextual question-answering, but still clearly occupies the physical-gathering personalization space.

4. US20070058041A1 / related family — “System and Methods for Enhancing the Experience of Spectators Attending a Live Sporting Event, with Contextual Information Distribution Capability.”
This is one of the best “live physical event” references. It explicitly covers delivering venue or event contextual information to handheld devices, including maps, washrooms, exits, schedule, team descriptions, and even profiles of drivers in the car-race example. Strong for contextual information at a live gathering; weaker on building participant profiles of the attendees themselves.

5. US7873710B2 — “Contextual data communication platform.”
This one is broader and less event-specific, but still relevant because it says device information combined with visitor profile information can personalize the services and information menu for a mobile user. It is useful if your claim language involves a mobile interface adapting information menus to a user’s profile in a physical setting.

6. US20130237240A1 — “Identifying meeting attendees using information from devices.”
This is a narrower match. It is about recognizing the physical presence of a mobile device near a physical meeting place and correlating that with an invitee-list profile. Strong on participant identification/profile matching at a physical gathering, but not as strong on delivering contextual information back to the attendee.

My blunt ranking for your phrasing:

Best overall: WO2020033354A3 family. It is the cleanest profile-driven in-venue personalization reference.

Best for location-aware venue guidance: US20180352373A1.

Best for live-event contextual content: US20070058041A1 family.

Best for attendee identification/profile linking: US20130237240A1.

What is not a tight match:

generic event-planning systems,

online meeting recommendation patents,

generic personalization patents not tied to a physical venue. The search turned up a lot of that noise, and I excluded it here.

If your real invention angle is participant profiles + contextual information requests + access-controlled responses at an in-person event, the first family is the one I’d inspect first, then the live-sport contextual-information family, then the attendee-identification patent.

query_text: Which of those explicitly receives a request for contextual information about another participant?
evaluation_goal: Optimized mode should stay within the retrieved set and surface the patent with direct contextual-information-request evidence.
baseline_answer:
I’m checking the claim language and detailed description for the “request” piece specifically. A lot of these patents personalize content, but that is not the same as explicitly receiving a request about another participant.

Thought for 22s
None of the physical-gathering patents we discussed clearly and explicitly say: a participant sends or makes a request for contextual information about another participant. The ones we looked at are mostly about attendee identification, profile use, personalization, access control, or contextual content delivery in general, not that specific request pattern.

More specifically, US20130237240A1 is about identifying attendees at a physical meeting place by correlating device information with profiles from an invitee list; it does not say one attendee requests contextual information about another attendee.

WO2020033354A3 is about personalizing a visitor experience at a venue using captured visitor information like location and demographics, but the visible description does not show a participant request about another participant.

US20070058041A1 is about distributing contextual information to spectators at a live sporting event, but that contextual information is event/venue-oriented, not framed as attendee A requesting information about attendee B.

US9659102B1 is about an event app that targets messages and provides real-time event information, but again, not that specific “request contextual information about another participant” behavior.

The closest near-miss I found is US20250292778A1, but it is really a meeting-content patent, not one of the physical-gathering venue patents. It detects attendees’ natural-language utterances, uses context associated with the first attendee and another attendee, and surfaces content items during the meeting. Even there, the language is still more like utterance-driven content recommendation than an explicit request for contextual information about another participant.

So the blunt answer is: none of those explicitly do that, based on the language I found. The best you can say is that US20250292778A1 comes closest functionally, but it is still not a clean explicit match.

query_text: patents about attendee customization and event schedule management at an event venue
evaluation_goal: Do the top patents concentrate on attendee customization, event schedules, and venue activity planning?
baseline_answer:
I’m separating two things that often get mashed together in patent results: attendee customization and schedule/session management at a venue. The useful hits are the ones that do both, not generic event software or generic personalization.

Thought for 15s
Best matches for attendee customization + event schedule management at an event venue:

1. US11532193B2 — “Multi-activity venue automation.”
This is the strongest hit. It explicitly says an itinerary management system can generate customized itineraries (schedules) for participants at multi-activity venues, and each itinerary can include times, timeslots, or time windows for activities at the venue. That is not vague personalization; it is direct attendee-specific schedule generation at a venue.

2. US20180352373A1 — “Personalized itinerary generation and mapping system.”
Also strong. It generates a personalized itinerary and map for a user within a venue, and the recommendations are based on user profile information plus point-of-interest information and the user’s location. This is more venue-navigation and itinerary personalization than formal session scheduling, but it is still a close match if your concept includes tailoring what an attendee should do and where they should go during the event.

3. US11337030B2 — “Assisted venue staff guidance.”
This looks like a continuation or adjacent family in the same venue-itinerary lane. It uses an itinerary associated with the venue attendee, tracks the attendee’s current path, and generates a suggested path to a point of interest from that itinerary. It also retrieves profile information about the attendee and uses that against point-of-interest traits to generate recommendations. This is good prior art if your claims involve dynamic itinerary guidance or staff intervention around an attendee’s personalized schedule.

4. US20060161549A1 — “Event management system.”
Older and broader, but still relevant. It says attendees at a convention can specify specific sessions, seminars, or programs, elect to receive specific promotional information or coupons, and view maps and general event-location information. It also references a master show schedule and attendee destination management. This is weaker than the top three because it reads more like event-management software than a deeply personalized venue engine, but it does combine schedule selection with attendee customization.

5. US9659102B1 — “Event application.”
Useful, but not as tight for schedule management. It is clearly about selective information delivery based on user profiles at event venues, and the app can filter menus, merchandise, and other event information by profile. It also supports venue-specific event information during the event window. The problem is that I did not find strong schedule-management language in the record I checked, so I would treat this as stronger on attendee customization than on scheduling.

Blunt ranking for your exact ask:

Best overall: US11532193B2
It directly covers customized attendee schedules at a multi-activity venue.

Best venue-personalized itinerary reference: US20180352373A1
Strong on profile-based itinerary and map generation inside a venue.

Best dynamic guidance follow-on: US11337030B2
Strong if your claims include live route correction or staff guidance tied to an attendee itinerary.

Best older broad convention-management reference: US20060161549A1
Covers attendee-selected sessions and event-program preferences, but it is broader and older.

What I would not overclaim:

US9659102B1 is not a clean “schedule management” patent from what I found. It is profile-filtered venue information delivery, not obviously attendee schedule orchestration.

Broad event-scheduling patents about organizers optimizing dates, venues, or meeting calendars are mostly the wrong lane unless your invention is organizer-side scheduling rather than attendee-side customization.

If you want the next useful cut, I can rank these by which is closest to “customized attendee schedule at a venue, plus contextual information delivery during the event.”

query_text: patents about invitation handling and visitor identification in a smart environment
evaluation_goal: Do the top patents anchor on invitation identification, visitor handling, and smart-environment actions rather than drifting into generic event software?
baseline_answer:
I’m targeting patents that combine two distinct pieces: invitation workflows and identifying who the visitor is once they show up in a smart/connected environment. A lot of results only do one of those.

Thought for 16s
The strongest matches I found are these:

US11049346B2 / SG11201907614YA — visitor access control with invitation-linked visitor profiles.
This is the cleanest fit if you want both invitation handling and visitor identification in a smart environment. The host system sends invitation data including an invitation ID and appointment time; the access-control system creates a visitor profile assigned to the invitation; and later the system receives the visitor’s image data, invitation ID, and device-specific identifier, then uses those to identify/control the visitor at the site. That is not just “smart door” stuff. It is invitation-driven identity handling in an access-controlled building/site.

US20130237240A1 — identifying meeting attendees using device information matched to an invitee list.
This is a good second match, especially for smart meeting rooms. The system recognizes a mobile device near a physical meeting place, retrieves identifying information from the device, and determines whether it matches a profile in an invitee list for a current meeting. If it matches, the profile can be included in the attendee list; if it does not, the system can prompt for approval or denial of inclusion. It also mentions notifications and personalizing settings. This is weaker on full invitation workflow than the patent above, but very strong on invitee-linked identification in a smart physical environment.

US8629755B2 — visitor management systems and methods.
This one is broader and more enterprise-security oriented. It covers identity management, registration, environment access control, surveillance, visitor enrollment and credential vetting, connecting the visitor to a destination or host, and disseminating visitor information. It is strong on smart-environment visitor handling, but it is not as explicit on invitation records being the key linkage for identification. So it is relevant, but not the closest if your invention really turns on invitation handling.

US20120044050A1 — smart doorbell visitor identification.
This is only a partial fit. It is clearly a smart environment patent and clearly about identifying a visitor before entry, using a badge or access card scanned at a smart doorbell, with notification to the resident. But it is basically a visitor-ID/alert patent, not a serious invitation-handling system.

What looks weaker or mostly noise:

US9978238B2 is a broad smart-home/security platform reference, not a tight invitation-handling + visitor-identification match.

US8065171B2 is about event planning, invites, RSVPs, and reminders, but not smart-environment visitor identification.

US7733213 is about meeting invitations plus time-limited visitor access passcodes, which is relevant on invitation/access, but the snippet I found is weaker on actual smart-environment visitor identification than the top two.

Blunt ranking:

US11049346B2 / SG11201907614YA — best overall.

US20130237240A1 — best for invitee-list-based identification in a smart meeting room.

US8629755B2 — best broad visitor-management/security system.

US20120044050A1 — more like smart-entry visitor ID than invitation handling.

If your actual target is narrower — for example, “host sends invite, visitor arrives, system recognizes visitor from device/image and links them to the invite” — then US11049346B2 is the one I would inspect first.

query_text: Which of those is strongest on visitor invitation handling rather than general event participation?
evaluation_goal: Optimized mode should keep the retrieved set and select the patent that is most clearly centered on visitor invitation handling.
baseline_answer:
I’m separating patents that are really about named visitors being invited into a site from patents that are just about attendees participating in an event. That distinction matters because a lot of “event” patents sound close but are the wrong lane.

Thought for 10s
US11049346B2 is the strongest on visitor invitation handling rather than general event participation.

Why: the patent is built around a host-generated invitation workflow. The access-control system receives invitation data from an electronic host system, including an invitation identification number and the appointed time when the host expects the visitor; it then creates a visitor profile assigned to that invitation and stores the invitation data in that profile. Later, the system receives the visitor’s image data and device identifier and links them back to the visitor profile by means of the invitation identification number. That is invitation-centric visitor handling, not generic attendee participation.

By contrast, US20130237240A1 is more about meeting attendee identification against an invitee list at a meeting place. It identifies attendees’ user profiles from device information and can match them to a profile in the attendee list, but the core idea is presence detection and attendee recognition, not a full invitation-driven visitor registration flow.

US8065171B2 is even broader and weaker for your question. It covers event planning tasks like inviting guests, tracking RSVPs, and reminders, but that is general event participation management, not visitor-specific invitation handling tied to identity and site entry.

US7733213 is the closest runner-up if your focus is invitation plus controlled entry: it describes electronically inviting meeting participants and generating a meeting-specific access passcode associated with the meeting invitation, including time-limited visitor access. But it still reads more like invitation-linked access credentialing than the fuller visitor-profile workflow in US11049346B2.

So the ranking is:

US11049346B2 — strongest on invitation-driven visitor handling.

US7733213 — strong on invitation-linked visitor access passcodes.

US20130237240A1 — more about attendee identification than invitation handling.

US8065171B2 — general event participation/admin.

If you are mapping prior art against a claim that starts with something like “receiving invitation data for a visitor, creating a visitor profile based on the invitation, and later identifying the visitor using invitation-linked data,” start with US11049346B2.

query_text: find patents similar to systems and methods for presenting information extracted from one or more data sources to event participants
evaluation_goal: Do the retrieved patents stay close to the event-participant contextual-information space so that the next turn can compare them cleanly?
baseline_answer:
I’m looking for patents that are not just generic event apps, but specifically involve extracting information from one or more data sources and presenting it to event participants. I’ll rank the closest matches, then separate strong matches from superficial ones.

Thought for 16s
Closest matches:

1. US10339504B2 / US20150379478A1 — “Systems and methods for presenting information extracted from one or more data sources to event participants.”
This is the direct hit. Same title as your phrasing. It is the strongest match by far because it is literally about presenting information extracted from one or more data sources to event participants.

2. US9659102B1 — “Event application.”
Strong second match. It says the app is associated with an event and ticket, receives event-related information from an event database, that database receives information from a plurality of sources, those sources are associated with APIs, and the app provides that event-related information to the user, with optional filtering based on a user profile. That is very close functionally, even if the title is broader.

3. US12335058B2 — “Automated generation of event participant information.”
Also relevant, but narrower in a different way. It triggers a search for participant information when there is a positive action for an event, gathers participant information from public, private, and software platform resources, resolves inconsistencies across sources, and transmits the resolved information to client devices for display. This is more about assembling participant information from multiple sources than general event information, but it is still a close prior-art neighbor.

4. EP2743869A1 — “Event management systems.”
Relevant but weaker. It says the system may obtain participant identity and availability data by querying participants’ personal information management applications and third-party systems, define event scenarios/options, and present those options to participants. This is closer to organizer-side event planning and proposal management than attendee-facing contextual information delivery.

5. US20250292778A1 — “Near real-time in-meeting content item suggestions.”
Conceptually related if your invention involves live contextual suggestions during an event. It monitors user activity information from multiple user devices and cloud-based services such as email, calendars, and social media, and uses contextual information associated with event transcripts or content. But this is really a meeting-content suggestion patent, not the cleanest “presenting extracted info to event participants” match.

Blunt ranking for similarity to your phrase:

US10339504B2 / US20150379478A1 — exact and strongest.

US9659102B1 — strongest practical analog based on multi-source event info delivery.

US12335058B2 — strong on multi-source participant info aggregation and display.

EP2743869A1 — more event-management than participant-facing info presentation.

US20250292778A1 — more meeting-context suggestion than event-participant info presentation.

The one you should inspect first is US10339504B2. The second one worth reading closely is US9659102B1. Those two are the most dangerous if you are testing novelty around multi-source event information being delivered to participants.

query_text: Walk me through the patent landscape for non-viral in vivo CRISPR adenine and prime editing delivered by ionizable lipid nanoparticles for allele-specific inherited retinal degeneration. Organize the answer under separate headings for pegRNA architecture, pegRNA stabilization, deaminase engineering, PAM-relaxed Cas variants, tissue-specific promoter choice, photoreceptor targeting, Muller glia targeting, intravitreal dosing, subretinal dosing, repeat dosing, biodistribution modeling, bystander edit control, immune evasion, manufacturing release assays, CMC scale-up, orphan-drug exclusivity, partnering strategy, reimbursement strategy, and freedom-to-operate outlook, and if evidence is missing for a heading, say that directly.
evaluation_goal: Does optimized mode clearly admit that the local event-tech patent pool is insufficient, while a single-prompt general LLM would be tempted to produce a polished but unsupported biotech patent list?
baseline_answer:
I’m mapping this carefully because you asked for a real patent landscape, not a generic CRISPR summary. I’m pulling patent families around retinal gene editing, LNP delivery, prime/adenine editing, and dosing/manufacturing, then I’ll flag where the record is actually thin instead of guessing.

The landscape is splitting into layers: core editing IP, ocular LNP delivery IP, and then a much thinner layer on actual retinal dosing/re-dosing and cell-type targeting. I’m digging into that thin layer now because that’s where people tend to overclaim.

Thought for 3m 27s
Bottom line
The patent landscape is component-dense but product-sparse. I found strong patent families for: core prime editing, pegRNA modifications, adenine base editors, PAM-relaxed Cas variants, ocular ionizable LNPs, and retina-disease-specific editing programs. What I did not find is a single clear family that already owns the fully integrated package of non-viral in vivo ABE/prime editing, ionizable LNP delivery, allele-specific inherited retinal degeneration, and photoreceptor-directed ocular dosing. In retinal disease, the most explicit allele-specific filings are still richer on nuclease knockout/replacement than on adenine or prime editing delivered by ocular ionizable LNPs.

pegRNA architecture
This is one of the clearer parts of the landscape. The core prime-editing family describes a prime editor as a programmable DNA-binding protein working with a polymerase, directed by a PE guide RNA that both targets the site and templates the desired edit through an engineered extension. The retina-specific CRB1 and PRPH2 prime-editing families then apply that architecture directly to inherited retinal disease and explicitly recite the classic spacer + PBS + RTT, with optional nicking guide RNA support.

For retinal programs specifically, the CRB1 and PRPH2 families matter because they move prime editing from platform IP into disease-anchored ocular claims. CRB1 is co-assigned to Columbia/Broad/Harvard, while PRPH2 appears under Columbia, which means the retinal prime-editing layer is already fragmented before delivery is even added.

pegRNA stabilization
This area is crowded. One family claims chemically modified pegRNAs for greater stability, longer activity, easier delivery into primary cells, and reduced toxicity; another claims pegRNAs with appended structural motifs such as aptamers, G-quadruplexes, tRNAs, or pseudoknots to increase editing efficiency, half-life in vivo, and intracellular lifespan.

That means a retinal prime-editing program using “improved” pegRNAs will likely run into separate pegRNA optimization IP, not just the core prime-editing patent. Ownership is also split: Agilent controls one chemical-modification family, while Broad/Harvard control a structural-motif family.

deaminase engineering
For adenine editing, the landscape is also layered. Beam has an ABE8 family that claims more efficient adenosine deaminase variants, including specific TadA-engineering features, while Broad/Harvard have families around ABEs and lower off-target behavior. The result is that “adenine editor” is not one patent bucket; it is a stack of platform and improvement claims.

For retinal relevance, UCSD has a disease-directed filing on inherited retinal disease base editing, including RPE65 correction and even non-viral delivery language, but that is disease-use IP, not the same thing as owning the ABE scaffold itself.

PAM-relaxed Cas variants
This is another likely choke point. The near-pamless / relaxed-PAM SpCas9 family claims engineered variants with relaxed PAM requirements, which matters a lot for allele-specific retinal editing because pathogenic variants often leave you with bad geometry for canonical NGG targeting.

The practical implication is simple: if your allele-specific retinal design depends on SpG/SpRY-like flexibility, you are probably stepping into an extra licensing layer on top of prime-editor or ABE IP. And if you combine relaxed PAMs with ABE8-style deaminases, the stack gets heavier, not lighter.

tissue-specific promoter choice
For a pure mRNA LNP product, promoter choice is often a red herring. The ocular LNP families I found are fundamentally about delivering mRNA / nucleic-acid cargo into the eye, and the core claim logic does not depend on a tissue-specific promoter in the way AAV or DNA vectors do.

Promoter IP still matters if you pivot to DNA payloads, hybrid systems, or viral adjuncts. There is now a photoreceptor-specific promoter filing, and there is also an older Müller-cell-specific promoter family. But for the narrow case you asked about—ionizable LNP delivery of adenine or prime editing—promoter patents are secondary unless your construct actually requires transcription from DNA after delivery.

photoreceptor targeting
This is one of the strongest non-viral retinal LNP pieces I found. Saliogen’s ocular LNP filing says subretinal SS-OP LNPs delivered reporter mRNA to about 60% of mouse neural retina cells within the treated area, with expression in the retinal layers that include photoreceptors, and also reports delivery into nonhuman primate photoreceptors and RPE after subretinal dosing.

There is also targeted-LNP language outside the eye-specific filings suggesting ApoE/ApoB-modified LNPs could exploit LDLR expression on RPE and photoreceptors, but that looks more like an enabling targeting concept than a clinically matured retinal editing package.

Muller glia targeting
Evidence is thin for ionizable LNP-mediated adenine or prime editing directed to Müller glia. I found Müller-glia-specific promoter IP and regenerative/reprogramming patents, but not a clean ocular ionizable-LNP editing family that specifically claims Müller glia as the main editable retinal target for ABE or prime editing.

So this heading is mostly a gap: the promoter and regeneration IP exists, but a retina-editing LNP program that clearly owns Müller-glia-targeted ABE/PE delivery was not obvious in the retrieved material.

intravitreal dosing
The ocular LNP record for intravitreal dosing is currently much stronger for anterior-segment / trabecular meshwork delivery than for photoreceptor rescue in inherited retinal degeneration. CRISPR Therapeutics’ ocular LNP family shows intravitreal delivery to trabecular meshwork with examples around 300 ng LNP in mouse eyes, but that is glaucoma-oriented, not a photoreceptor-editing IRD program.

Older ocular lipid nanoparticle work does report intravitreal retinal transfection and even suggests intravitreal injection was more efficient for incorporating nanoparticles into retinal cells in that setting, but it is not a modern ionizable-LNP CRISPR retinal-editing package.

subretinal dosing
Subretinal delivery is where the retinal non-viral LNP claims look most credible. Tufts/Mass Eye and Ear explicitly claim local eye administration including subretinal injection, and Saliogen’s filing gives the strongest experimental detail: subretinal injection in mice, measured tolerability by fundoscopy/OCT/IHC/ddPCR/RT-qPCR, 1 µL per eye in one mouse study, and NHP subretinal delivery with 75 nm particles and <0.2 PDI.

UCSD’s inherited retinal disease base-editing filing also repeatedly frames retinal base editing through subretinal injection, at least in the RPE65 examples. So subretinal is the route with the most concrete patent support for retinal editing today.

repeat dosing
Evidence is missing for a strong retina-specific repeat-dosing patent position in ocular ionizable LNP editing. I found repeat-dose language in a broader non-viral capsid-free DNA family, including weekly or bi-weekly administration, but not a clear retinal ABE/prime-editing LNP family built around ocular re-dosing.

That is important because repeat dosing is one of the conceptual advantages people often claim for non-viral systems over AAV. In the retrieved patents, that advantage is more often asserted generically than nailed down with ocular IRD-specific claims and data.

biodistribution modeling
Evidence is missing for true biodistribution modeling IP specific to this use case. The patents I found are mostly empirical: they show where expression appears after ocular dosing, compare treated versus untreated areas, and quantify delivery to retina, RPE, or trabecular meshwork. That is useful, but it is not a serious modeling layer.

The closest thing to a design-oriented targeting angle is targeted-LNP work such as ApoE/ApoB-modified particles for RPE/photoreceptor opportunity, plus generic LNP patents that say biodistribution can be determined in vivo. That is still not the same as owning a retinal biodistribution model or simulation framework.

bystander edit control
For adenine editing, this is an active IP area. Broad/Harvard families emphasize low unwanted by-products and reduced off-target effects, while newer patent families expressly claim reduced RNA editing, reduced bystander editing, or context-specific control of bystanders. ABE8-related materials also describe reduced bystander mutations relative to older editors in some embodiments.

For prime editing, the retrieved patent record is much thinner on “bystander control” as a separate claim bucket. The pegRNA-improvement families are mainly about efficiency and stability, not a retinal-specific bystander-control program. So this heading is strong for ABE, weak for retinal prime-editing LNPs.

immune evasion
There are two relevant layers here. First, there is explicit CRISPR IP on immune-orthogonal / immune-stealth Cas9 strategies for avoiding anti-Cas immune responses. Second, there are LNP patents claiming reduced inflammatory exacerbation or improved immune behavior for LNP formulations.

But the retinal-specific non-viral ABE/prime-editing ocular patent set is not yet rich on immune-evasion claims. The retrieved ocular LNP patents focus much more on delivery and tolerability than on a dedicated immune-stealth retinal-editing package.

manufacturing release assays
This is not retina-specific, but there is real manufacturing IP here. The retrieved LNP patents and analytical-method filings support a release / control toolbox built around particle size, PDI, encapsulation efficiency, RNA integrity, lipid–RNA adduct monitoring, and potency assays based on in vitro expression. Examples include RiboGreen for encapsulation, RP-IP HPLC and capillary electrophoresis for RNA integrity/adduct burden, LC-MS/MS for adduct chemistry, and expression assays after extracting LNP RNA.

For ocular programs specifically, the Saliogen filing also makes 75 nm size and <0.2 PDI part of the disclosed in vivo NHP-ready formulation picture, which is the kind of physical-specification data that usually ends up in release criteria.

CMC scale-up
The CMC scale-up patents are mostly platform LNP manufacturing rather than retina-specific. The strongest recurring theme is controlled nanoparticle formation by rapid mixing—mixing chambers, tangential fluid impact, or comparable instant-formation approaches—to lock down size and encapsulation reproducibly at scale.

So the CMC conclusion is blunt: there is enough general LNP process IP to make scale-up a real FTO issue, but not much in the retrieved set that is uniquely tailored to ocular prime/ABE LNP manufacturing. The headache is platform CMC access, not retina-specific CMC novelty.

orphan-drug exclusivity
This is a regulatory issue, not a patent issue. In the U.S., orphan designation can bring tax credits, fee relief, and a potential 7 years of market exclusivity after approval. In the EU, orphan medicines get 10 years of market exclusivity, with a possible 2-year pediatric extension under the stated conditions.

For allele-specific inherited retinal degeneration, orphan incentives are highly relevant because patient populations are tiny. But they do not solve the patent stack. They help commercial protection after approval; they do not remove the need to license the core editing and delivery IP.

partnering strategy
The smart partnering strategy is not “one big pharma deal first.” It is license stacking plus retinal execution. A credible program may need access to: core prime editing from MIT/Broad/Harvard; pegRNA stabilization from Broad/Harvard and possibly Agilent; ABE/ABE8 rights from Beam and/or Broad-linked families; PAM-relaxed Cas9 from MGH; ocular LNP rights from CRISPR Therapeutics, Tufts/Mass Eye and Ear, or Saliogen; and disease-specific retinal editing claims from UCSD, Columbia, or Broad/Mass Eye and Ear depending on target and modality.

So the best BD posture is probably: lock down one disease, one route, one modality, then partner outward. For example, a subretinal photoreceptor program with either prime editing only or ABE only is cleaner than trying to combine ABE, prime editing, relaxed PAMs, and novel pegRNA scaffolds on day one. The IP gets unmanageable fast.

reimbursement strategy
There is no retina-specific reimbursement patent moat here. The useful precedent is market access for Luxturna: NICE accepted it under a highly specialized technology pathway and notes a simple discount patient access scheme, while CMS has billing/coding support and local coverage infrastructure for subretinal administration.

For a future non-viral retinal editor, the likely reimbursement play is the same one the broader gene-therapy market is moving toward: argue one-time or infrequent durable benefit, use tight biomarker-anchored eligibility, and be prepared for outcomes-based agreements. CMS’s current CGT Access Model explicitly describes agreements where payment is tied to patient outcomes and manufacturers reimburse payers if outcomes disappoint.

freedom-to-operate outlook
Preliminary view only: FTO looks hard, but not impossible. The risk is not one killer patent; it is the stack. Foundational CRISPR rights, prime-editing core IP, pegRNA-improvement patents, adenine-editor patents, PAM-relaxed Cas variants, ocular LNP delivery patents, and target-specific retinal disease filings all sit in overlapping layers owned by different institutions and companies.

The cleanest FTO path would likely be: pick one modality; avoid promoter dependence by staying with RNA LNP payloads where possible; avoid optional IP layers unless they are truly necessary; and build around a subretinal photoreceptor-first use case, because that is where the non-viral retinal LNP evidence is strongest. The weakest areas in the current patent set are Müller-glia editing, retinal repeat dosing, and formal biodistribution modeling, which could be opportunity zones as much as risk zones.

If you want, I can turn this into a claim-chart style map with columns for owner, modality, route, target cell, and likely blocking relevance.
